coredata.dc:description: The selective oxidation of hydrocarbons (RH) by dioxygen (O2) is a very important method for industrial production of bulk oxygen-containing compounds, However, due to the high chemical inertness of RH and O2, achieving this reaction under mild conditions still faces significant challenges. This paper discloses an efficient hybridizing engineering (HE) strategy of decatungstate (DT) with 3d transition metal ions (Mn+=Ni2+, Co2+ or Cu2+)-modified carbon quantum dots (Mn+/CQD) as the dopants. Compared to pure CQD, The Mn+/CQD can more efficiently combine with DT anion to fabricate a high-quality hybrid via electrostatic interactions, thereby bestowing the hybrid with an enhanced visible light response especially the separation efficiency of photo-generated charge pairs. Furthermore, the above hybridization effects of Mn+/CQD on DT anion can be fine-tuned and gradually improved with a change of the metal ion from Cu2+, Co2+ to Ni2+, along with a continuous enhancement of the hybrid's photo-catalytic efficiency in the visible light- triggered selective oxidation of ethylbenzene with O2 in acetonitrile. The best Ni2+/CQD-doped TBADT can achieve ca. 48% ethylbenzene conversion and 87.6% acetophenone selectivity in the presence of 2 M HCl, and it also shows a much higher photo-catalytic activity for the photo-oxidation of toluene, cyclohexane and benzyl alcohol compared to pure TBADT. The HE strategy with Mn+/CQD as the cationized hybridizers is much superior to that with pure CQD or Ni2+ as the hybridizer in hoisting the photo-catalytic performance of DT.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The selective oxidation of hydrocarbons (RH) by dioxygen (O2) is a very important method for industrial production of bulk oxygen-containing compounds, However, due to the high chemical inertness of RH and O2, achieving this reaction under mild conditions still faces significant challenges. This paper discloses an efficient hybridizing engineering (HE) strategy of decatungstate (DT) with 3d transition metal ions (Mn+=Ni2+, Co2+ or Cu2+)-modified carbon quantum dots (Mn+/CQD) as the dopants. Compared to pure CQD, The Mn+/CQD can more efficiently combine with DT anion to fabricate a high-quality hybrid via electrostatic interactions, thereby bestowing the hybrid with an enhanced visible light response especially the separation efficiency of photo-generated charge pairs. Furthermore, the above hybridization effects of Mn+/CQD on DT anion can be fine-tuned and gradually improved with a change of the metal ion from Cu2+, Co2+ to Ni2+, along with a continuous enhancement of the hybrid's photo-catalytic efficiency in the visible light- triggered selective oxidation of ethylbenzene with O2 in acetonitrile. The best Ni2+/CQD-doped TBADT can achieve ca. 48% ethylbenzene conversion and 87.6% acetophenone selectivity in the presence of 2 M HCl, and it also shows a much higher photo-catalytic activity for the photo-oxidation of toluene, cyclohexane and benzyl alcohol compared to pure TBADT. The HE strategy with Mn+/CQD as the cationized hybridizers is much superior to that with pure CQD or Ni2+ as the hybridizer in hoisting the photo-catalytic performance of DT.

A novel hybridizing engineering strategy of non-lacunary decatungstate (DT) with transition metal ions-modified carbon quantum dots as dopants has been developed to remarkably improve the photocatalytic performance of DT in visible light-triggered oxidation of hydrocarbons by molecular oxygen in MeCN under mild conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The selective oxygenation of hydrocarbons (RH) by dioxygen (O2) or air is the most important conversion technology for industrial production of bulk oxygen-containing compounds. Which is also significance for theoretical study of saturated C(sp3)-H bond selective oxidation [1]. The thermal catalysis oxidation is highly appreciated owing to a low cost and an environmental friendliness, but it usually needs to use the harsh operating conditions (high temperature and pressure) to efficiently activate the inert RH and O2, which easily leads to the relatively low efficiency. Photo-catalysis has been increasingly applied to the selective oxidation of RH by O2 because the photo-generated electron (e-)-hole (h+) charge pairs from the irradiated catalyst with ultraviolet light visible or light can reduce O2 and oxidize the C(sp3)-H of RH, respectively, thus realizing this selective oxidation under very mild conditions. However, most photo-catalysts commonly show a lower oxidation efficiency than the thermal catalysis systems mainly due to their weak visible light response and especially fast recombination of charge carriers. Some efficient techniques such as doping with carbon materials or transition metals, construction of heterojunction structures with Metal-Organic Frameworks and so on have been widely used to enhance the photo-catalytic efficiency of the semiconductors [2-4].

The selective oxidation of ethylbenzene (EB) to acetophenone (ACP) is one of the important conversions for the development of petrochemical downstream products and fine chemicals [5-7] and considerable research efforts for this selective oxidation have focused on the thermal catalysis technology using various heterogeneous catalysts [8-15]. Differencing from the harsh operating conditions (high temperature and pressure) of thermal catalysis. Photo-catalysis can efficiently and selectively achieve the oxidation of RH by O2 and has been successfully used to the synthesis of ACP from EB oxidation with O2 [ 16-19]. To choose a catalyst with better photo- sensitivity and photochemistry structural stability is particularly important in photo- catalytic field. As one of the most efficient photo-catalysts reported so far, decatungstate (DT) anion especially its tetrabutyl ammonium salt (TBADT) has been deeply researched [20-22] and extensively applied in the photo-catalytic synthesis, especially including the photo-catalytic oxidation of inactive hydrocarbons by O2 [23-28]. Study on the improvement of DT's photo- catalytic eggingric has expanded to new extents, like loading DT on supports [29,30],using acidic additives as the promoters [31] and counter cations as the modifiers [18]. However, the inborn defects of TBADT, which includes a low synthetic quality and an unstable structure, especially poor visible light harvesting efficiency and unsatisfied photo-excited state lifetime. Impedes the improvement of its photo-catalytic efficiency. Some efficient hybridizing engineering (HE) strategies of POMs with photosensitizer, metal substitution and ligand (substrate) coordination have been developed to significantly improve the photocatalytic performance [32], which are highly desirable to address the shortcomings of DT as a visible-light-responsive catalyst.

Carbon quantum dot (CQD) attaching polar carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [33] and sulfonic acid groups [34] on its surface has some unique attributes such as low cost and toxicity, broad optical absorptions, tunable fluorescence emissions, excellent physicochemical and photochemical stability and so on [35,36]. Due to the above characters of CQD, it has been extensively applied to chemical sensing, biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [37,38]. Especially, the excellent electron-accepting and -transport behaviors of CQD can remarkably reduce to the recombination of photo-generated charge carries. So that CQD, as an ideal dopant, has been extensively applied to improve the photo-catalytic efficiencies of semiconductor oxides such as TiO2 [39], Bi2WO6 [40], Cu2O [41], BiOX [42], TBADT [43] and other metal oxides [44-46]. More importantly, the photo- physiochemical properties of CQD can be further fine-tuned through the modification of various metal ions [47-50]. On the other hand, the substitution or doping of transition metal ions may improve the quality of POMs and extend metal-to-POMs charge transfer and excited-state lifetimes [51,52]. Especially, some sandwich type POMs that are formed by sandwiching metal ions between two missing units, become more stable than their initial structures and their redox potentials can be controlled at the molecular level by tailoring their structures and constituent elements and/or by the introduction of additional metal cations into the POM framework [32], thereby render them an unique catalysis performance in the epoxidation of olefins with H2O2 [53,54] or N2O [55].

Inspired by the above-mentioned research progresses of CQD-hybridized semiconductor oxides and metals-hybridized POMs, recently, two effective HE strategies with CQD [43] and transition metal ions [56] as the dopants have been successfully developed by our groups to remarkably improve the photo-catalytic performance of DT in the visible light-driven selective oxidation of organic compounds with O2. In order to fully utilize the respective advantages of these two dopants in improving the synthesis quality of TBADT and especially its physiochemical properties, herein, we would report that the use of transition metal ions-modified CQD (Mn+/CQD, Mn+= Co2+, Ni2+or Cu2+) as the formal co-dopants to further improve the photo-catalytic performance of TBADT. A series of characterizations and photo-catalysis experiments support that the doping of Mn+/CQD can render TBADT with a better synthetic quality, structure stability, stronger redox capacity and higher visible light harvesting efficiency, especially more excellent separation efficiency of the photo-generated charge pairs compared to pure CQD. Moreover, the above hybridization effects of Mn+/CQD can be fine-tuned at the molecular level by the modified metal ions. Due to the above unique hybridization attributes, Mn+/CQD-doped TBADT catalysts exhibit a far higher photo-catalytic performance for the visible-light-driven selective oxidation of EB and other organic compounds by O2 compared to CQD-doped TBADT and especially pure TBADT. The newly developed HE strategy can not only comprehensively resolve the inherent defects of TBADT in visible light catalysis applications, but also provide a new way to fine-tune the photo-physiochemical properties of TBADT at the molecular level.

All chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Sodium tungstate (Na2WO4·2H2O), tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBABr), nickel acetate (NiAc2·4H2O), cobalt acetate (CoAc2·4H2O), copper acetate (CuAc2·H2O), ethylenediamine, citric acid, ethylbenzene, cyclohexane, toluene, benzyl alcohol, acetonitrile (MeCN) were purchased from Sinopharm and used without further treatment. Distilled water was used throughout this experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of metal ion modified N-containing CQD
ce:section.ce:para: Metal ion modified N-containing CQD (Mn+/CQD) solid was fabricated according to the improved Zhu et al.’s previous work followed by freeze-drying [33]. In general, 10 g citric acid and 3.5 mL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water, then 1.7 mol Mac2 (M=Co2+, Ni2+or Cu2+) was add to this solution, the resulting solution was transferred into 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h After cooling to room temperature, the carbonized solution was dialyzed for 24 h to get obtain a purified solution. Finally, the purified solution was dealt with freeze-drying to obtain Mn+/CQD solid. For comparison, pure N-containing CQD solid was also fabricated in the absence of metal salts according to the above-mentioned operating procedure. FT-IR spectra in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information (SI)) indicated that CQD has its characteristic absorption peaks that are assigned to the C = O stretching vibrations of carboxyl and amide at 1710 and 1635 cm-1 as well as the bending vibrations of NH at 1570 cm-1 [33]. After modification with the above metal ions, these characteristic peaks become weak or shifted, supporting the coordination of CQD's carboxyl and amide groups with the modified metal ions. Additionally, Photo-luminescence (PL) spectra of CQD and Mn+/CQD aqueous solutions in Fig. S2 revealed that CQD can emit a strong PL signal at a central wavelength of 450 nm under 320 nm light excitation, the intensity of this PL signal is remarkably attenuated after modification with Ni2+, Co2+ and especially Cu2+ and it's red-shift also appears at Co2+/CQD and especially Ni2+/CQD, supporting a transfer of the photo-generated electrons of CQD to the modified metal ions.

Mn+/CQD-hybridized TBADT catalysts were synthesized using the same fabrication method of CQD-hybridized TBADT previously reported by us [43]. Typically, 0.01 mol TBABr and 3 wt% Mn+/CQD were dissolved into 6 mL deionized water under ultrasonic wave, then, the resulting solution was transferred to a 10 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and treated at 100 °C for 24 h under autogenous pressure to obtain the TBA+ micelle-encapsulated Mn+/CQD solution, which was marked as solution A. Next, 6.4 g (19.4 mmol) Na2WO4·2H2O was dissolved with 40 mL water, followed by the addition of 13.4 mL of 3 M HCl aqueous solution, the acidified solution was heated to boiling for 5–10 min to obtain a decatungstate acid solution (marked as solution B). The above solution A was added to the solution B at 100 °C under continuous stirring and the brown solid was gradually precipitated from the reaction solution. After cooling, the obtained precipitate was filtered, repeatedly washed with water and ethanol and then dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h to yield the goal catalyst (Mn+/CQD-TBADT). For comparison, two control samples TBADT and CQD-TBADT were also fabricated using the above synthesis method. The Co2+, Ni2+and Cu2+ contents of Mn+/CQD-TBADT catalysts measured by ICP method were 0.012, 0.011 and 0.010 wt%, respectively. Additionally, by use of the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of the samples in air and 20–950 °C (Fig. S3), the practical doping amounts of CQD, Ni2+/CQD in TBADT were estimated to be ca. 3.0 and 2.0 wt%, respectively, which could indicative of the introduction of Ni2+/CQD dopant into TBADT.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the samples was measured on a VG Multi Lab 2000 system with a monochromatic Mg-Kα source operated at 20 kV. Liquid UV–vis spectra of the samples in MeCN were recorded from 200 to 800 nm on UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the solid samples were recorded from 200 to 800 nm using an UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-3310) with BaSO4 as a reference. Transmission FT-IR spectra of the samples were recorded from 400 to 4000 cm−1 on a Nicolet Nexus 510 P FT-IR spectroscopy using a KBr disk. Photo-luminescence (PL) measurements of the samples in MeCN were carried out on fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-7000) at room temperature. Electrochemical characterization includes transient photoelectric current (TPC), electro- chemical impedance spectra and cyclic eggingric (CV) experiments of the samples in MeCN were measured with an electrochemical workstation (CHI 650e Chenhua Instrument Company). The preparation method of working electrode was described as follows: 40 mg catalyst was ultrasonically suspended in 2 mL acetonitrile for 20 min, and subsequently 30 µL Nafion was added to make a stable slurry. Then, dropping 400 μL slurry and naturally drying to form a uniform thin film on ITO sheet. The prepared working electrode has an effective illumination area of 1 cm2. A 300 W Xe lamp provided a visible light source through a cut-off filter (420 nm) for the determination of photocurrent. Thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) of the samples was performed on a NETZSCH-STA 409 PC at 20 °C/min heating rate under a flowing air atmosphere (10 mL/min).

In 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution with a bias potential of 0.3 V, the photoelectric current curve, impedance, Mottschottky curve and current density voltage curve of samples were measured by CHI 660E three-electrode system. Using platinum plate as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The preparation method of working electrode was described as follows: 40 mg catalyst was ultrasonically suspended in 2 mL acetonitrile for 20 min, and subsequently 30 µL Nafion was added to make a stable slurry. Then, dropping 400μL slurry and naturally drying to form a uniform thin film on ITO sheet. The prepared working electrode has an effective illumination area of 1 cm2. A 300 W Xe lamp provided a visible light source through a cut-off filter (420 nm) for the determination of photocurrent.

Visible-light-catalytic oxidation experiments were conducted on a self-designed common glass photo-reactor equipped with an ethanol (0–5 °C)-cooled condenser and an oxygen storage vessel (1 atm). A 35 W tungsten–bromine lamp purchased from NVC lighting corporation (With an UV light filter, Light intensity, 535 mW/cm2; Operating voltage, 12 V) was used as a built-in light source. The whole lighting reaction was operated in the closed reactor under normal temperature and pressure and its specific operating conditions and analytical method for the oxygenated products were measured via GC analysis which could be found in our recent publication [31]. The conversion of organic substrate and yield of the oxidative products were calculated by the internal standard method with cyclohexanone as the internal standard. The actual consumption of substrate and the actual production of target products were expressed in terms of their molar quantity (in mol), the conversion of substrate, the yield of target product and its selectivity were calculated by the following equations:

TEM: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CQD-TBADT and Ni2+/CQD-TBADT were obtained from a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV and the results are shown in Fig. S4 and Fig. 1 , respectively. The TEM images of these two samples obtained using insoluble absolute ethyl alcohol as a dispersion medium were very similar to each other, presenting the dense stacking morphology of rice-like nanoparticles (NPs), with a certain amount of 2–5 nm black CQD NPs scattered on the surface. The sizes of the rice-like NPs were between 1.5 and 2.0 nm, which basically corresponded to the size of the single cage of TBADT. When soluble MeCN was selected as a dispersion medium, the above rice-like NPs presented a complete dispersion state in the TEM images of the two samples and some black NPs dispersed in TBADT matrix were found in the TEM images (Fig. 1). Notably, the dispersion of black CQD NPs in the matrix showed a slight agglomeration, with CQD NPs mainly distributed in the range of 2–4 nm, but also producing aggregates of 10–18 nm (Fig. 1a). The Ni2+/CQD NPs in Fig. 1b displayed a better dispersion in TBADT matrix than CQD NPs and were mostly concentrated in the range of 2–5 nm, supporting that the Ni2+/CQD can be better doped in TBADT, which may be due to the enhanced electrostatic effect between the modified metal cations and DT anions.

FT-IR: Fig. 2 a illustrates that the FT-IR spectra of TBADT, CQD-TBADT and Mn+/CQD-TBADT hybrids were very similar to each other, consisting of the characteristic peaks in 500–1000 cm-1 and 1200–3000 cm-1, which are respectively attributable to the [W10O32]4− anions and tetrabuthyl-ammonium cations. Three characteristic peaks of TBADT appeared at 968, 900 and 804 cm-1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of its W=Ot, W-Ob-W and W-Oe-W bonds, respectively, which can be indicative of the structural character of the [W10O32]4− [31]. The magnified view (Fig. 2b) in 500–1250 cm-1 clearly shows that these three peaks were hardly influenced by the doping of CQD or Mn+/CQD, indicating that the current hybridizing strategy can well maintain the structure of DT anions.

XRD: Powder X-ray diffraction analyses of TBADT, CQD-TBADT and Ni2+/CQD-TBADT were conducted on a Rigaku 2550 X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.15406 nm) and a graphite monochromator, operated at avoltage of 40 kV, a current of 250 mA and a slit width of 0.15 mm. XRD patterns (Fig. 3 ) were collected in the angular range of 5–30° with a scanning rate 2°/min. Compared with the strong diffraction peaks of TBADT (7.5°, 8.0°, 9.3° and 9.9°), the characteristic diffraction peaks at 9.3° and 9.9° in the XRD pattern of the CQD or Ni2+/CQD -doped samples were almost completely disappeared. Notably, compared to TBADT the two diffraction peaks at 7.5° and 8.0° in Ni2+/ -TBADT and especially CQD-TBADT exhibited a remarkably decrease in the intensity, along with an obvious shift toward low angle region. These findings indicate that the doping of CQD causes a sharp reduction in the crystallinity of TBADT accompanied by a slight change in its crystallization behavior, and its unfavorable effect on the crystallinity could be significantly slowed down by introducing Ni2+ modifier.

XPS: The chemical states for the surface atoms of pure TBADT and typical hybrid Ni2+/CQD-TBADT was investigated by X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) and the high resolution XPS spectra for the surface W and O atoms of these two samples are shown in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4a, the two energy peaks for W4f5/2 and W4f7/2 in TBADT appeared at 37.06 and 34.94 eV, respectively, the doping of Ni2+/CQD caused these two peaks to take place a shift of about 0.5 eV to higher energy regions. Simultaneously leading to an energy peak at 531.17 eV for the O1 s of TBADT being shifted to a low energy region of 530.41 eV (Fig. 4b). This indicates that the doping of Ni2+/CQD can induce electronic deviation from W to O atoms, thus resulting in an increase of the binding energy (BE) of W atom and a decrease of the BE of O atom. Fig. S5 illuminates that, in addition to the XPS signals of W, O, C, N atoms attributed to TBADT, no XPS signal of Ni atom was found in Ni2+/CQD-TBADT, likely owing to its very low content in this hybrid.

UV–vis spectra: Fig. 5 a displays that the UV–vis spectra of CQD and Mn+/CQD aqueous solutions exhibited two characteristic bands about at 240 and 340–350 nm, which are respectively assigned to π-π* transition of an polycyclic aromatic C = C systems of CQD and n-π* transition of its C = O or other related groups [57]. Notably, the modification with transient metal ions resulted in a significant enhancement in the intensity of these two bands of CQD, along with a red-shift (about 4–10 nm) of the n-π* band. And the intensity of this n-π* band was regulated by the modified metal ions in CQD, presenting a egging increase from Cu2+, Co2+to Ni2+, along with a strengthened visible absorpation in 400–500 nm. Inset of Fig. 5a displays that CQD solution was yellowish and could emit a blue fluorescence under UV light illumination, the color of its fluorescence emission was almost unchanged after modification with the trasition metal ions. The liquid UV–vis absorption spectra of TBADT and Mn+/CQD-TBADT hybrids in MeCN were also carefully studied and the recorded spectral curves are shown in Fig. 5b. The UV–vis spectra of these samples were very similar to each other, all displaying two characteristic bands in 250–350 nm, in agreement with the previous works [58,59]. A band at 325 nm is assigned to oxygen to tungsten charge transition (LMCT) of four linear W-O-W bridge bonds in the [W10O32]4− structure [58,59], and another weak band at 265 nm originates from a LMCT process of the unstable structural subunit [W5O16]2− [60] or the egging structural [H2W12O40]6− [61]. Notably, CQD and Mn+/CQD dopants did not affect the centered wavelength of these two LMCT bands, and their characteristic bands were hardly found in the doped samples as they overlapped with those belonged to TBADT. According to our previously reported method [18], the synthetic quality of these catalysts was evaluated based on a ratio (R = I325 nm / I265 nm) for the integral areas of the above two LMCT bands and the calculated R values are listed in Table 1 . It can be seen from Table 1 that the obtained R values for pure TBADT, CQD-TBADT and Mn+/CQD-TBADT (Mn+=Co2+, Cu2+, Ni2+) hybrids were ca. 0.72, 0.77, 0.74, 0.75 and 0.78, respectively, indicating that the synthetic quality of TBADT closely relative to photo-catalytic performance is improved by the CQD-based dopants, especially including Ni2+/CQD. By using the literature proposed method [62], the enlarged UV–vis spectra of Fig. 5a in 360–440 nm were treated to obtain the energy bandgap (Eg/eV, See Table 1). Fig. 5b gives the treated spectral curves of three Mn+/CQD-TBADT hybrids and the treated spectral curves of TBADT and CQD-TBADT could be found in Fig. S6. It can be seen from Table 1 that the Eg values of Mn+/CQD-TBADT hybrids in MeCN were slightly lower than those of TBADT (3.06 eV) and CQD-TBADT (2.99 eV), presenting a gradual reduction with a change of the modified metal ion from Cu2+(2.97 eV), Co2+(2.95 eV) to Ni2+ (2.92 eV), supporting that the modified transition metal ions can fine-tune and enhance the sensitization effect of CQD on TBADT, which is closely related to the improved visible light harvesting efficiency. By the way, the above Eg values were basically consistent with those obtained by using the solid UV/Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) [63] (See Fig. 6 a and b and Table S1).

We previously reported that, under light illumination, TBADT in pure MeCN was unstable and prone to degradation to other non-photoactive subunits [18,31]. The stabilizing effect of Mn+/CQD dopants on the structural band of TBADT at 325 nm under the condition of simulating real photo-reaction was also investigated using three representative samples TBADT, CQD- TBADT and Ni2+/CQD-TBADT and the recorded UV–vis spectra are shown in Fig. 5d and Fig. S7, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5d, the structural band of Ni2+/CQD-TBADT at 325 nm in MeCN still maintained well after 12 h of visible light irradiation (Curves 2 vs 1) and could be slightly strengthened when water as a good stabilizer for TBADT [64] was introduced into the irradiated solution (Curve 3), which could be indicative of an excellent anti photo-degradation performance of this hybrid. Also, the irradiated CQD-TBADT in MeCN and especially MeCNH2O exhibited an improved stability compared to TBADT (see Fig. S7), but under the same illumination conditions, it's stability was slightly inferior to that of Ni2+/CQD-TBADT. These findings support the enhanced stabilization effect of CQD on the illuminated-TBADT can be better inherited after modification with Ni2+ ions. By the way, in the presence of 2 M and especially 12 M HCl solution, a slight blue-shift and an obvious decay of the structural band were observed in the UV–vis spectra of these irradiated catalysts (Curves 4–5 in Fig. 5d and Fig. S7), which should be due to the serious hydrolysis of TBADT in the strong acid solution [43].

UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra :The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra were used to analyze the optical absorption properties of TBADT, CQD-TBADT, and Mn+/CQD-TBADT (Mn+=Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+). The corresponding DRS curves for these materials are shown in Fig. 6. Pure TBADT had minimal absorption response in the UV–vis spectrum within the wavelength range of 400–800 nm. Nevertheless, the spectral absorption signal of CQD-TBADT exhibited a substantial increase across the wavelength range of 400–750 nm, accompanied by a notable red shift in the absorption signal. An absorption band within the wavelength range of 445–480 nm was observed in CQD-TBADT, which can be explained by the π-π conjugation effect resulting from the C = N double bonds in the CQD structure. Following the alteration of metal ions, the absorption intensity of Mn+/CQD-TBADT hybrids in the range of 400–750 nm presented a gradual decline with the metal ion modifier from Cu2+, Ni2+to Co2+, but their intensities was still stronger than that of pure TBADT. The relationship between the absorption coefficient (α) and the incident photon energy (hv) can be expressed as αhν=C1(hν-Eg) [65], where C1 represents the constant for light absorption and Eg denotes the band gap energy of the photocatalyst. The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of TBADT, CQD-TBADT and Mn+/CQD-TBADT (Mn+=Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+) were obtained using the formula and the results are shown in Fig. 6. The band gap energy values of these catalysts were 3.07, 2.96, 2.90, 2.85 and 2.92 eV, respectively. This demonstrates that the band gap energy of TBADT doped with Mn+/CQD is commonly lower than that of TBADT doped with CQD alone. Additionally, the band gap energy of CQD-TBADT may be tune-regulated by varying metal ions, with a decreasing trend from Cu2+, Co2+ to Ni2+. The above characteristic results indicate that the visible light harvesting efficiency of TBADT can be improved by the sensitization of CQD and Mn+/CQD dopants, thus bestowing the hybrids with an enhanced visible light catalytic performance, as supported by the results of the subsequent light reaction experiments.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra: PL spectral technique, as a forceful tool to evaluate the stability (or lifetime) of the photo-excited state [66], was used to study the photo-catalytic systems of TBADT and its hybrids containing CQD or Mn+/CQD. As shown in Fig. 7 a, a PL spectrum of TBADT displayed a strong and broad PL peak at 350–550 nm in MeCN under 320 nm light excitation (Curve 1), while the doping of CQD in TBADT caused this PL signal to be significantly attenuated by more than half (Curve 2). It is worth noting that the PL signals of Mn+/CQD-TBADT catalysts presented a more severe attenuation compared to those of CQD-TBADT [43] and Mn+ -TBADT [56] hybrids previously reported by us, which were hardly observed at the current ordinate scale (Curves 3–5). An enlarged view thereof displays that this negligible PL signal was gradually enhanced with varying the modified metal ion of CQD from Co2+, Cu2+ to Ni2+and its splitting at 430 and 472 nm was also observed upon hybrid catalysts with the CQD modified by Co2+, especially by Cu2+ or Ni2+ (see Inset of Fig. 7a). The strong fluorescence quenching effect appeared at these hybrid catalysts support that owing to the excellent electron-accepting and -transport behaviors of CQD [67], its doping can significantly stabilize the photo-excited state of TBADT via accepting the photo-generated electrons. More importantly, the modified metal ions on CQD perhaps play a relay function in accelerating a transfer of the photo-electrons from TBADT to CQD as their cationized and coordinated effects perhaps significantly enhance the interactions between CQDs and DT anions. The influence of the additives on the PL intensity was further investigated using Ni2+/CQD-TBADT as a representative sample, as shown in Fig. 7b, the PL intensity of this hybrid was slightly enhanced in the presence of water but reduced in the presence of 2 M and especially 12 M HCl, which was consistent with our previously reported results in pure TBADT [31] and CQD-doped TBADT [43] photo-catalysis systems.

Frontier orbital energy levels: According to the literature suggestion method [54], the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of TBADT, CQD-TBADT and Mn+/CQD-TBADT hybrids in MeCN were recorded under anhydrous anaerobic conditions at a scan rate of 100 mV/s with ferrocene /ferrocenium couple as standard [68](−0.02 V shown in Fig. S8). Fig. 8 gives the CV curves of these samples. Two pairs of strong redox waves in −2.0 - −0.8 V could be found in the CV curves of all the samples. One pair of redox waves at −0.8 - −1.4 V originates from the electrolyte TBAHFP. Another pair at −1.6 - −1.8 V should be attributable to the redox process between W6+and W5+ions. The oxidation potential (EOx) for DT anion was recommended to estimate the HOMO energy by the calculation formula (- [EOx –(−0.02)+4.8]) proposed by literature [62], and the corresponding LUMO energy was also estimated by the sum of the HOMO energy and the above measured bandgap energy (Eg). Table 1 lists the data for the EOx, HOMO and LUMO energy levels of these samples. In that, the HOMO energy levels of the hybrids were all slightly higher than that of pure TBADT and presented a gradual increase with varying the dopant from CQD, Ni2+/CQD, Co2+/CQD to Cu2+/CQD. And Their LUMO energy levels were reduced to some extent compared to that of pure TBADT and the maximum reduction in LUMO energy level occurred upon Ni2+/CQD-TBADT. This indicates that the frontier orbital energy levels of TBADT are slightly influenced by the CQD-based dopants and can be fine-tuned by the modified metal ions of dopants. Notably, Fig. S9 and Table S1 reveal that the redox capacities of the hybrids in MeCN-2 M HCl media were stronger than that of TBADT and could be slightly fine-tuned by their dopants, presenting an increasing sequence of Ni2+/ CQD > Co2+/ CQD > Cu2+/ CQD > CQD.

Transient photoelectric current (TPC) and Electrochemical impedance spectra: The need of effectively separating electron-hole pairs for improving photo-catalytic activity was widely recognized. Generally, a higher photocurrent value signifies a superior level of separation efficiency between electrons and holes. Consequently, the photocurrent measurement and the corresponding electrochemical impedance were conducted on the work electrodes coated with TBADT, CQD-TBADT, and Mn+/CQD-TBADT. Fig. 9 a showcases that when the work electrode coated with those catalysts was exposed to a 300 W xenon light, it displayed a positive TPC peak as a result of its n-type conductivity. Compared to pure TBADT, the hybridized TBADT catalysts commonly showed stronger photocurrent responses and the TPC signal in the hybrids was gradually enhanced with the change of dopant from CQD, Cu2+/CQD, Co2+/CQD to Ni2+/CQD, supporting that the CQD and especially the transition metal ions-modified CQD can decrease the recombination rate of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Fig. 9b further shows that the impedance radiuses of these hybrids were smaller than that of TBAD, presenting a decreasing sequence of CQD-TBADT > Cu2+/CQD-TBADT > Co2+/CQD-TBADT > Ni2+/CQD –TBADT, This further supports that the accelerating effect of CQD and especially Mn+/CQD on the transfer of photo-generated charge carriers of TBADT. Based the present PL, TPC and EIS characteristic results, among the hybrids, Ni2+/CQD-TBADT displayed the exceptional separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers and was expected to the best photocatalysis performance, as supported by the subsequent photoreaction evaluation results.

The photo-catalytic performances of TBADT, CQD-TBADT and Mn+/CQD-TBADT catalysts were evaluated using the selective oxidation of ethylbenzene (EB) with molecular oxygen (O2) in MeCN under visible light illumination and the results are listed in Table 2 . TBADT, as we previously reported [31], exhibited a low catalytic activity for this photo-oxidation, only achieving 7% EB conversion with 70% acetophenone (ACP) selectivity under sustained exposure to visible light for 12 h (Entry 1). The use of CQD-TBADT achieved 11% EB conversion and 85% ACP selectivity under the same reaction conditions (Entry 2), which can reflect a promoted effect of the doped CQD on the photo-catalytic performance of TBADT. Entry 3 shows that under the photo-catalysis of Ni2+-TBADT, EB conversion was enhanced to 16.9%, but the selectivity of ACP was reduced to 73%. More importantly, Entries 4–6 reveal that the doped effect of CQD was improved remarkably after modification with 3d transient metal ions, thus resulting in the conversion of EB was doubled approximately, along with a slight improvement of ACP selectivity. Notably, the photo-catalytic performance of Mn+/CQD-TBADT was adjusted by the modified metal ions, presenting a gradual increase with a change of the metal ion from Cu2+, Co2+ to Ni2+. The best Ni2+/CQD-TBADT could achieve ca. 27.2% EB conversion and 81.5% ACP selectivity and the conversion increased by about three times compared to pure TBADT, representing the huge potential of the current HE strategy. The effect of water and various acidic additives on the photo-oxidation of EB with O2 were also investigated using the best Ni2+/CQD-TBADT as a catalyst. As shown in Entries 7–8, this photo-catalytic oxidation could be further improved in the presence of water and especially 12 M HCl solution, providing 29.1 and 33.9 % EB conversions, respectively. The highest 39% EB conversion and as high as 84% ACP selectivity were obtained in a mixed solution of the above two additives (tagged 2 M HCl, Entry 9) and further extending illumination time to 24 h could increase EB conversion and ACP selectivity to 48.1 and 87.6%, respectively (see data in brackets for Entry 9). Among the other acidic additives examined, the weakly acidic 2 M Hac and moderately acidic 2 M H3PO4 solutions (Entries 10–11) were far inferior to 2 M HCl in improving EB conversion, while 2 M H2SO4 and PhSO3H solutions with a similar strong acidity to 2 M HCl, also exhibited a good promoted effect to this photo-catalysis oxidation (Entries 12–13).

Next, the influence of various variables on the photo-oxidation of EB catalyzed by Ni2+/CQD-TBADT was examined and the obtained data are shown in Fig. 10 a–d. It can be seen from Fig. 10a that when the concentration of catalyst was between 0.3 and 0.9 mol %, EB conversion in pure MeCN presented an uninterrupted and rapid increase from 9 to 26% with the concentration, along with a continuous increase in ACP selectivity from 68 to 79% and a gradual decrease in α-phenyl alcohol (α-PEA). After that, the effect of hoisting catalyst concentration became insignificant owing to its solubility becoming worse, only achieving ca. 30% EB conversion and 82% ACP selectivity at 1.5 mol % concentration. Fig. 10b displays that the conversion in MeCN-2 M HCl media continuously and remarkably ascended from 15 to 39 % with extending irradiation time from 6 to 12 h, along with a slight improvement in ACP selectivity. Thereafter, a further extension of the time to 24 h resulted in a slow increase in EB conversion to ca.48% and a slight improvement in ACP selectivity. Fig. 10c illuminates the effect of HCl amount on photo-catalytic oxidation, when the amount of HCl rose from 0.12 to 1.2 mmol, EB conversion ascended from 29 to 42%, and ACP selectivity was improved from 74 to 86%, which should be due to the enhanced redox capacity of catalyst under protonation of HCl [31]. After that, the conversion was slightly reduced with the further increase of HCl amount, which may be caused by the degradation of catalyst under light irradiation, as supported by the UV–vis results mentioned above. Fig. 10d displays that when the addition amount of water was enhanced from 0 to 0.7 mL, EB conversion continuously rose from 34 to 40%. Thereafter, an attempt to further enhance water amount to 1.2 mL contrarily resulted in a significant reduction in EB conversion and ACP selectivity, along with an increase in α-PEA selectivity. This is likely because an excess amount of water causes the solubility of catalyst to become worse and the catalyst precipitated from the solution seriously affects the transmittance of visible light.

The stability of TBADT and Ni2+/CQD-TBADT photo-catalytic oxygenation systems were compared by the irradiation time-dependence of EB conversion. As are shown in Fig. 11 . the irradiation time-dependence of EB conversion in these two photo-catalytic systems had a similar regularity whether the additive 2 M HCl was present or absent. Namely, EB conversion presented a relatively fast increase in the former stage of photoreaction (about 6–21 h) and then turned into a slow increase in the latter stage of photo-reaction (21–36 h). Notably, the hybrid catalyst in MeCN was obviously superior to TBADT in the rate and sustainability of EB conversion growth (Curves 3 vs 1) and its advantage in hoisting the conversion could be further amplified in the presence of 2 M HCl (Curves 4 vs 2). These results indicate that the doping of Ni2+/CQD remarkably enhances the photo-catalytic activity of TBADT but also improves its stability during photoreaction.

Finally, the photo-oxidation of other substrates (1 mmol) such as cyclohexane (CYH), toluene (PhMe) and benzyl alcohol (BA) under the same conditions as EB photo-oxidation was also used to examine the effectiveness of Ni2+/CQD-TBADT. As shown in Table 3 , this catalyst also showed a good activity for the photo-oxidation of these substrates, under its photo-catalysis, about 12% PhMe, 23% CYH and 74% BA were converted to the corresponding oxygenated products in pure MeCN, mainly including BA and benzaldehyde (PhCHO) for PhMe, cyclohexanol (CY-OH) and cyclohexanone (CY=O) for CYH and PhCHO and benzoic acid (PhCOOH) for BA. These three photo-catalytic oxidations were also promoted dramatically in the presence of 2 M HCl solution, leading to the conversions of PhMe, CYH and BA being increased by approximately 8, 12 and 9 %, respectively. These results were much superior to those previously reported in pure TBADT [31] and CQD-TBADT [43] photo-catalysis systems.

To gain insight into the current photocatalytic oxidation mechanism, the possible primary active species oxygen vacancies (holes, h+) and superoxide anion radicals (O2 •-) in the photo- oxidation of EB with O2 were examined using sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) and para-benzoquinone (BQ) as the quenchers of h+ and O2 •-, respectively. As shown in Fig. S10 and Fig. 12 , whether TBADT or Ni2+/CQD-TBADT was used as the photo-catalyst, the photo-oxidation of EB by O2 was nearly completely quenched in the presence of Na2C2O4 (1.2 eq. addition amount relative to EB). It is noteworthy that the addition of 1.2 eq. BQ could partly quench the photo-oxidation catalyzed by Ni2+/CQD-TBADT but reversely resulted in an increase in EB conversion in the pure TBADT photo-catalytic system, which should be due to the catalytic contribution of BQ itself to the photo-oxidation, as supported by the blank experiment of only using BQ as the photo-catalyst (see Table S2). The above quenching experiments support that the photo-generated h+ should be the most key active species for the present photocatalytic oxidation [69-70], while the reduction of O2 by the photo-generated e- to the O2 •- species seems to hardly occur on the pure TBADT but could proceed limitedly in the relay of the doped Ni2+/CQD.

In summary, we have developed a novel and efficient hybridizing engineering (HE) strategy for improving the photo-catalytic performance of TBADT, which has the following merits: i) The inexpensive 3d transition metal ions (Mn+)-modified CQD (Mn+/CQD) can be conveniently fabricated by one step hydrothermal carbonization, which are the better dopants than CQD for the preparation of hybridized TBADT. Ii) the dopant Mn+/CQD can tune-regulate and improve the synthesis quality, redox capacity and visible light harvesting efficiency of TBADT at the molecular level, especially including the separation efficiency of the photo-generated charge carriers. Iii) Due to the above favorable hybridization effects, the Mn+/CQD-doped TBADT hybrids show a significantly enhanced photo-catalytic performance in the selective oxidation of hydrocarbons by O2 under visible light illumination and their photo-catalytic efficiency can be further amplified in the presence of 2 M HCl solution. With such kind of robust HE strategy in hand, we are interested in further exploring the synthesis of TBADT-based hybrid catalysts using other transient metal and rare earth metal ions single or co-modified CQD dopants and their potential applications in other important visible-light- catalysis reactions.
